prompt:
Production monitoring for the Python gateway reported a sudden increase in active time series. The captured samples in `incident/metric_samples.jsonl` all describe successful GETs handled by the same route, but every request produced a distinct `http_request_duration_seconds` series. Investigate those samples and repair `RequestMetrics` so request-duration series use bounded dimensions without losing the trace exemplars that make slow observations diagnosable.

Keep the public API unchanged. A series must be identified by exactly `method`, `route`, and `status_class`: preserve the existing finite method and status-class normalization, use a configured router template for `route`, and collapse missing or unconfigured templates to `unmatched`. A raw request target (including path identifiers or query strings) must never become a metric label. Observations with the same bounded dimensions must aggregate their count and total duration. A sampled observation must update that series' exemplar with its trace ID and observed duration; a later observation without a trace ID must not erase the useful exemplar. Do not move trace IDs into labels, modify the incident evidence, or modify the protected tests.

The suite is fully offline and deterministic. Run `PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 PYTHONHASHSEED=0 python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p 'test_*.py' -v`.
verify_cmd: env PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 PYTHONHASHSEED=0 python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p 'test_*.py' -v

full_distill.primary_skill: infer a metric-cardinality failure from captured samples and replace a raw request label with bounded request dimensions while preserving trace exemplars
full_distill.baseline_evidence: the incident snapshot contains six series with identical method and status class but six distinct raw request labels; the protected suite reproduces that fan-out because RequestMetrics keys series by RequestObservation.target
full_distill.success_contract: requests for a configured route aggregate under method, route template, and status class; unmatched targets share one fallback series; sampled trace IDs remain exemplar metadata and unsampled observations do not erase the last useful exemplar
adversarial_factors: concrete path identifiers and query cursors differ on every captured request even though the router template is the same
an observation may claim an unconfigured route template and must not create a new series
unknown methods and out-of-range status codes must retain their existing bounded fallback buckets
a trace ID is intentionally unique and useful, but only as exemplar metadata rather than a series label
an unsampled observation follows a sampled one in the same series and must not clear its exemplar
